description: Monday 15 December | Social Services and Community Sub-Committee A Redress System for Abuse in Care Bill Hearing of evidence: Citizens Advice Bureau Sacha Green, National Advisor - Legal & Strategic Mana Mokopuna – Children’s Commissioner Dr Claire Achmad, Children’s Commissioner Network for Survivors of Faith-based Schools in Te Waipounamu Charitable Trust Taria Brown Max Marshall
descriptionSummary: A. Redress System for Abuse in Care Bill. Citizens Advice Bureau

Um, so citizens of Vice Bureau, hopefully, you know, our organization. We have 80 sites around the country, over 2000 volunteers delivering free information and advice by phone, um, online and face-to-face. Um, we provide free service, um, to people across Al Turo.

And CAB is a place that survivors people with lived experience in their whanau come to get support with issues impacting their lives. These inquiries cover a diverse range of topics and include, um, most notably redress as an issue that people come to us seeking help for.

Um, we also deliver an information advice service to people in prison with 23 of our ca AABs answering, um, calls directly from all corrections facilities and like all frontline CAB service. Um, that service receives no direct central government funding.

Uh, we also seek to work alongside survivor led groups and to be informed by the views of survivors from our mahi. We are aware of the challenges and barriers people face in knowing and acting on their rights. Um, we support the establishment of a fear accessible and survivor centered redress system for people who experienced abuse when they should have been being cared for by the state and by faith-based institutions.

Given our commitment to a fair, accessible, and survivor centered red system, we cannot support this bill. As it stands, the redress system is an opportunity for the crown to uphold the mana of survivors and come with humility and recognition of failure.

Failure that warranted a national apology by the crown just over a year ago, to take steps to acknowledge and respond to the harm survivors experienced to restore their dignity and wellbeing and provide meaningful remedies in a trauma-informed way. Instead, this bill focuses on creating barriers to financial, redress, penalizing people, and protecting the state from being held to the genuineness of an apology at a later date, we oppose this bill and urge the committee to recommend significant redrafting to ensure the system reflects the intent

of the Royal Commission of Inquiry and the expectations of survivors. Um, I also just want to note the timing and process of consultation. Hopefully the committee received our letter. I know it was a little bit late in the piece for anything, um, to change, but the timing of, um, consultation on this bill lining up with the National Day of, um, reflection and remembrance meant that many, um, survivors, um, were limited in their ability to engage fully with this process.

And it may not have even been on their radar 'cause they were, um, taking that time to prepare for, um, major events around the country throughout November. So we do urge the committee to consider reopening, um, the consultation process to allow for genuine and accessible engagement.

Um, our key concern in this bill is the presumption against financial redress for survivors who have committed serious violent or sexual offenses. We oppose this measure and submit that it is unjust and discriminatory to make access to redress conditional on a person's criminal history rather than on the state's wrongdoing.

The shift's focus from recognizing harm caused and care to judging survivors worthiness of compensation that contradicts the Royal Commission of Inquiries recommendations, which explicitly call for redress the redress system to include survivors with criminal histories. Um, further the, um, this presumption breaches the Bill of Rights Act and, um, as an example of double jeopardy, where people who have already been penalized and served their time face further punishment, um, by being limited in their access to financial redress

that can have an impact for them and for their, for their children, for their whanau. Um, the retrospective application of the presumption to claims made on or after 9th of May is also problematic, especially given the poor communication about the changes and the barriers survivors already face in accessing information.

Um, the messaging already has caused confusion with some agencies implying that there is a blanket ban for any person who has, uh, criminal record. Um, in our work with people in prison, we see the barriers, um, that people face, uh, in, um, accessing the system as it stands, including, um, accessing redress that they're entitled to.

Um, this is about limited access to personal records, digital exclusion, um, difficulties in communication. So we would like to see a trauma informed system that addresses these practical barriers and does not compound them. We are also concerned about the bill's provisions preventing apologies given in the redress context from being used as evidence in civil proceedings.

Um, while the intention is to, to encourage meaningful apologies, this may weaken, weaken survivors access to justice limiting their ability to rely on such apologies where they choose to pursue civil remedies. The, um, presumption also disproportionately impacts Maori who are both overrepresented in care and in the criminal justice system.

This outcome is inconsistent with Article three of TE or TGI and undermines commitments to equitable outcomes. So we've, we've provided extensive, um, recommendations for what we think should happen to prov, um, to create a meaningful redress system that is accessible fear, trauma informed and centered on survivors, and we encourage the committee to, um, recommend that the bill is, um, rewritten to align with these purposes.

I'm an independent Crown entity and I lead my team at Manana Children's Commissioner this evening. young people and adults were abused and neglected right here in New Zealand by the very people who were responsible for protecting them when they came into their care. This number equates to nearly 40% of those in care over this time, having been harmed tonight, I wish to again acknowledge all of those survivors in mohu, those who are living today and those who have passed on.

I want to acknowledge the courage and strength of all of those almost 3000 survivors in Mahu who shared their testimonies of abuse, violence, and harm and associated profound detrimental impacts that they have experienced and those survivors and Mohu who did not get to share their experiences with the Royal Commission.

As Children's Commissioner, I pay close attention to the findings of the Royal Commission of, um, inquiry. I pay close attention to them because they are a chilling and urgent reminder of the power that the state and institutions entrusted with the care of children and young people hold and wield.

These findings are a reminder of the urgency with which we must work collectively every day to ensure that these harms do not continue to occur for children and young people in the care of the state and institutions. Today. The findings of the Royal Commission will continue to underpin my independent advocacy for all children and young people in the care and or custody of the state today and tomorrow as a designated national preventative mechanism under the optional protocol to the Convention against torture.

I am very mindful of the role that together with my team at Manam Moana, I play to provide one aspect of oversight today of the systems across the state care and youth justice facilities, youth mental health, forensic facilities, and youth intellectual disability facilities with the aim of preventing harm for children and young people in this work, the findings of the Royal Commission of Inquiry Loom large serving as a constant reminder of the necessity of the work that we and other op cap national preventative mechanisms

and other parts of the wider oversight system are doing. The Royal Commission of Inquiry made very clear findings that Maori Pacific Disabled neurodiverse, mentally distressed and rainbow people were overrepresented in experiencing abuse and care.

As children's commissioner, I am very, very mindful of the fact that abuse and harm and state care is a contemporary and ongoing issue right now with hundreds of ANA in state care still experiencing abuse, violence, and neglect annually. Today in the year 2025, we must face up to the fact that Una Maori, Ana Maori, and Pacific and Pacific are still overrepresented in experiencing harm in the state care and protection system.

I share these reflections with you tonight and in the context of this bill, because they highlight the fact that where the state perpetuates harm on its citizens, on the children, and young people in its care, it has failed in its duties to those children and young people.

It follows as a central tenant of the law that the state and institutions who have caused this harm must then fulfill it. Its and their duties to provide redress to those children and young people. And at its heart, that is what this belt is about.

In the words of Ahi, who has experienced harm in the care of the state in more recent times, words that were shared in the position paper published last year, and I quote more than anything, we need accountability. That when failures are glaring and recommendations are made to fix the failures that these recommendations are acted on, evidenced and maintained.

As children's commissioner, I strongly support the words of that ranta. I urge that legislation to provide a redress system in response to the findings of the Royal Commission of Inquiry must uphold the full findings of the Royal Commission and the intent of the recommendations that have been made.

This is crucial to provide holistic redress for survivors in mahu and to ensure that the intergenerational impacts of abuse and care are acknowledged. Acknowledged. As many of the witness testimonies of survivors in Mohu before the Royal Commission highlighted the impacts of abuse in state care, they are not purely historical.

They continue today because these impacts, they are deeply intergenerational. The trauma, social dislocation and harm reverberate through children and young people today, hanau and entire communities. The opportunity of getting this legislation right and ensuring a holistic centered scheme, this opportunity that we have as a nation right now, it is a profound opportunity.

I think it is really well summed up in this quote from a care experience. Ranga Tahi who in 2023 stated when talking about the reform of the redress system stated, and I quote again, a different ran. This is a once in a lifetime opportunity for us to recognize the failures of the state and to acknowledge the lifelong and intergenerational effect that this has had on survivors of abuse.

The state has harmed generations of my hanau, and this is a chance to bring some form of justice and recognition. I entered the care of the state at age of five, and I left at 17. I began my advocacy career the moment I left the system. I am very concerned as children's commissioner about the message that this bill in its current form, sends to survivors, mahu and children and young people who are in the care of the state.

I urge that this bill should not proceed in its current form. It fails to deliver a comprehensive, holistic redress system that survivors and the Royal Commission of Inquiry have called for and recommended. I am very concerned about the discriminatory impact of the bill on Mahu Maori and their whanau, and that the bill does not acknowledge the realities as laid bare in the Royal Commission's final report of the direct connection between abuse and the care of the state and criminal offending later in life.

For some mahu and survivors, I urge that all survivors in mohu must receive redress without discrimination and that a system is established with urgency. This is needed not only to provide redress for historical harms caused by the state and faith-based institutions, but to ensure a system that is robust on an intergenerational basis, acknowledging the reality that harm and the care of the state is still continuing today, despite the fact that all children in our country have a basic right to grow up safe, free from all forms
